rule.name: abundance
rule.message: Use enough or plenty, or give specific amount.

rule.name: accurate
rule.message: If it is not about aiming, use right; also detailed or correct.

rule.name: all things considered (omit)
rule.message: Remove as unnecessary.

rule.name: along the lines of (like)
rule.message: Did you mean like?

rule.name: as a matter of fact (in fact, actually, omit)
rule.message: Replace with less wordy in fact or actually, or remove completely.

rule.name: as per (as, in accordance with)
rule.message: Business jargon meaning in accordance with. Replace with as or modify the sentence.

rule.name: at all times (always)
rule.message: Simply, always. Pay attention to place in the sentence.

rule.name: at your earliest convenience (as soon as you can)
rule.message: Overly formal. Did you mean as soon as you can?

rule.name: belated (late)
rule.message: Outdated. Did you mean late?

rule.name: by means of (by, with, through)
rule.message: Did you mean by, with or through?

rule.name: currently
rule.message: Omit or replace with now

rule.name: wordy phrase: due to the fact
rule.message: Remove wordy "\1 \2 \3 \4 \5" because

rule.name: for all intents and purposes (omit)
rule.message: See if you could remove this phrase.

rule.name: for the most part (omit)
rule.message: See if you could remove this phrase.

rule.name: for the purpose of (to, for)
rule.message: Did you mean to or for?

rule.name: in a manner of speaking (omit)
rule.message: See if you could remove this phrase.

rule.name: in a very real sense (omit)
rule.message: See if you could remove this phrase.

rule.name: in my opinion (omit)
rule.message: See if you could remove this phrase.

rule.name: in terms of (in, for)
rule.message: Change to in or for.

rule.name: in the affirmative (yes, affirmatively)
rule.message: Change to yes or affirmatively.

rule.name: in the case of (about, to)
rule.message: Change to about or to and change the word order of the sentence.

rule.name: in the nature of (like)
rule.message: Did you mean like?

rule.name: in the neighborhood of (about)
rule.message: Can be used as almost, approximately, more or less, around. Often about.

rule.name: in the process of
rule.message: Possibly empty phrase "\1 \2 \3 \4" when it means "currently".

rule.name: majority (most, usually) when not voting
rule.message: Majority in the sense of "more than half" is used with countable nouns only (esp. when voting); for uncountable nouns, use most or usually.

rule.name: not able (unable)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean unable?

rule.name: not certain (uncertain)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean uncertain?

rule.name: not many (few)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean few?

rule.name: not the same (different)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean different?

rule.name: not unlike (similar, alike)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean similar or alike?

rule.name: on the occasion of (on, when, because of)
rule.message: Did you mean on? Try also when and because of.

rule.name: on the other hand (omit, but, yet)
rule.message: Avoid completely or try but, yet

rule.name: on two separate occasions (twice)
rule.message: Did you mean twice?

rule.name: sooner rather than later (soon, now)
rule.message: Try soon or now meaning sooner than expected.

rule.name: sufficient (enough)
rule.message: Use enough.

rule.name: that exists (omit)
rule.message: Try removing the phrase.

rule.name: the undersigned (I, me, we)
rule.message: Legalese. Did you mean I, me or we?

rule.name: type of (omit)
rule.message: Try removing the phrase "\1 \2".

rule.name: until such time as (until)
rule.message: Use until.

rule.name: you are requested (please)
rule.message: Replace with please

rule.name: in excess of (more than, over, exceeding)
rule.message: Better with more than, over, or exceeding

rule.name: accede to (give in)
rule.message: Unless it is a throne that is acceded to, change into allow, agree to or give in to.

rule.name: accelerate
rule.message: Technical. Elsewhere replace with speed up.

rule.name: accentuate
rule.message: Replace with stress or emphasize.

rule.name: accommodation
rule.message: room.

rule.name: accompany
rule.message: If not in musical context, use go with or with.

rule.name: verb accomplish (do, archive, finish)
rule.message: Try one of: do or achieve or finish.

rule.name: verb accrue
rule.message: Use add or gain.

rule.name: verb acquire (get, develop)
rule.message: A simple get is enough most of the time, sometimes develop.

rule.name: assist, assistance (help)
rule.message: Did you mean simpler help?

rule.name: be advised
rule.message: Remove wordy "\1 \2"

rule.name: Possible wordiness: be a X one
rule.message: \1 \3 may be more concise.

rule.name: clearly, obviously
rule.message: Something that is clear to you is not always clear to the reader. Remove "\1" unless it is necessary.

rule.name: each and every (every)
rule.message: Replace with \3 \4

rule.name: have a tendency (tends)
rule.message: Did you mean tends?

rule.name: have the ability to (be able to, can)
rule.message: Did you mean can?

rule.name: in the event of, in the event that (if, in case)
rule.message: Did you mean if or in case of?

rule.name: in the final or last or ultimate analysis (finally, in conclusion)
rule.message: Did you mean finally or in conclusion? You could remove the phase, too.

rule.name: it seems that (omit)
rule.message: Possibly empty phrase "\1 \2 \3"

rule.name: make an attempt/effort/try (try)
rule.message: Can you write \1 and still be correct?

rule.name: make decisions about (decide on)
rule.message: Did you mean decide on?

rule.name: not accept (reject)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean reject?

rule.name: not often, not very often (seldom. rare, rarely)
rule.message: Avoid using "not". Did you mean seldom, rare or rarely?

rule.name: obtain (get)
rule.message: Did you mean simpler get?

rule.name: Legal jargon referring to previous text
rule.message: Legalese. Bad style in any normal text. Remove and explain. Use this or these.

rule.name: regard as being (regard as)
rule.message: Word "being" is redundant. Use \1 \2.

rule.name: solicit for
rule.message: Avoid. Use asking for.

rule.name: take action to (omit)
rule.message: Remove as verbose.

rule.name: Starting sentence with 'The truth/fact is'.
rule.message: Remove. Just say what the truth or fact is.

rule.name: what I mean to say is that (omit)
rule.message: Try removing the phrase.

rule.name: with/in reference to, with/in regard to (about, of, on, for, concerning, regarding)
rule.message: Replace with shorter about, of, on, for, concerning, or regarding.

rule.name: worthwhile is weak
rule.message: Weak word. Use something stronger like valuable.

rule.name: accused of a charge (charged with)
rule.message: This phrase may be redundant. Consider writing: charged with instead.

rule.name: adequate enough (adequate)
rule.message: This phrase might be redundant. Consider using \1.

rule.name: there are also other (also)
rule.message: Consider using there are other or there are also

rule.name: as of yet (yet)
rule.message: Consider using yet or as yet.

rule.name: autobiography of a life (autobiography)
rule.message: Consider using \1 or story \2 \3 \4 instead.

rule.name: bald-headed (bald)
rule.message: Consider using bald.

rule.name: because of the fact that (because)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using because.

rule.name: bitter in taste (bitter)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: bivouac camp (bivouac)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider writing \1.

rule.name: circumstances surrounding (circumstances of)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider writing \1 of.

rule.name: directly antithetical (antithetical)
rule.message: This phrase verges on redundancy. Consider writing \2.

rule.name: each and every one (each one)
rule.message: Consider using \1 \4.

rule.name: established fact (fact)
rule.message: Consider using \3.

rule.name: every now and then (now and then)
rule.message: Consider an alternative to strengthen your wording. sometimes

rule.name: on a few occasions (occasionally)
rule.message: Consider using occasionally

rule.name: honest truth (truth)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: Mount Fuji
rule.message: As “Yama” means “mountain” in Japanese, better write Mount Fuji?

rule.name: of any of → of
rule.message: Consider simply using of instead.

rule.name: originally born in (born in)
rule.message: Consider using \2 \3.

rule.name: point being is that (point is that/point being that)
rule.message: Consider using \1 \3 \4 or \1 \2 \4.

rule.name: reason why (reason)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: right over (over)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \2.

rule.name: rustic country (rustic)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: salsa sauce (salsa)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: self-admitted (admitted)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using just admitted.

rule.name: a small number of (a few)
rule.message: Specify a number, remove phrase, use a few, or use some

rule.name: so as to (to)
rule.message: 'So as to' expresses purpose and is used in formal texts. Consider using to.

rule.name: so therefore (therefore)
rule.message: Consider using \1 or \2.

rule.name: subject matter (subject)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \1 to avoid wordiness.

rule.name: subsequent to (after)
rule.message: Consider using after

rule.name: sufficient enough → sufficient
rule.message: 'Sufficient enough' is redundant. Consider using only sufficient.

rule.name: temporary bivouac (bivouac)
rule.message: This phrase may be redundant. Consider writing \2.

rule.name: therapeutic treatment (treatment)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: will in the future (will)
rule.message: Consider using will or will eventually.

rule.name: with the exception of (except)
rule.message: Consider using except or except for

rule.name: work colleague (colleague)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: Big in size, yellow in color, etc.
rule.message: This wording could be more concise. \1

rule.name: all of the (all the)
rule.message: Consider removing "of" to be more concise \1 \3

rule.name: approximately about
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \1.

rule.name: biography of her life (biography)
rule.message: Consider using \1 or story \2 \3 \4 instead.

rule.name: CDROM (CD-ROM)
rule.message: Consider using CD-ROM.

rule.name: CD disc (CD)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: combine together (combine)
rule.message: '\1 \2' is redundant. Use \1

rule.name: comfortable with doing (comfortable doing)
rule.message: Consider using \1 \3.

rule.name: commute back and forth (commute)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: continue to remain (remain)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: descend down (descend)
rule.message: This phrase might be redundant. Consider using \1.

rule.name: famous celebrity (celebrity)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: fellow classmates/co-workers/comrades (classmates/co-workers/comrades)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: female actor (actress)
rule.message: Consider using actress.

rule.name: female waiter (waitress)
rule.message: Consider using waitress.

rule.name: first man president (male president)
rule.message: Use male.

rule.name: first woman president (female president)
rule.message: Use female.

rule.name: focus in (focus on)
rule.message: In this context, 'focus' is usually used with the preposition on.

rule.name: foreign import (import)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Use simply \2.

rule.name: free gift
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \2.

rule.name: gather up (gather)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \1.

rule.name: general public (public)
rule.message: Consider using only \2 to avoid wordiness.

rule.name: help to find (help find)
rule.message: In informal texts, 'to' is not necessary. Do you mean \1 \2 \4?

rule.name: introduce for the first time (introduce)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: might perhaps (might)
rule.message: Consider using \1, \2.

rule.name: numerous different (numerous)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: past experience/memory (experience/memory)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: private industry (industry)
rule.message: This phrase is redundant. Consider using \2.

rule.name: repeat again (repeat)
rule.message: Consider using \1.

rule.name: Rio Grande (river)
rule.message: '\1' means river. Simply use \1 \2.

rule.name: send an email to (email)
rule.message: Consider using email.

rule.name: sent an email to (email)
rule.message: Consider using emailed.

rule.name: I [told it to] -> I [told] Leo
rule.message: This phrasing could be wordy. \1

rule.name: fall season (fall)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: try and (try to)
rule.message: "Try and" is common in colloquial speech, but try to is recommended for writing.

rule.name: very unique (unique)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: whole lot (lot)
rule.message: Consider using \2.

rule.name: Collocation: addiction to/of
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1" is "to". Did you mean \1 to \3?

rule.name: Collocation: advertisement of/for
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "for", not "\2". Did you mean advertisement for?

rule.name: Collocation: afraid from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: Collocation: analysis about/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "analysis" is "of". analysis of

rule.name: Collocation: angry against/with
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "with" or "at", not "\2". Did you mean \1 with or \1 at?

rule.name: Collocation: ashamed from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: Collocation: at/on the job
rule.message: The usual collocation is on the job or at work.

rule.name: Collocation: careful for/with
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "with", not "\2". Did you mean \1 with?

rule.name: Collocation: damage of/to
rule.message: The usual collocation for the word "damage" is "to", not "of". \1 to

rule.name: Disappointed with, at, or by
rule.message: The usual collocation for "disappointed" is "with", "at", "by", but never "of". Did you mean \1 with?

rule.name: Collocation: far of/from
rule.message: The usual preposition for "far" is "from". far from

rule.name: Collocation: fed up of/with
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1 \2" is "with". Did you mean \1 \2 with?

rule.name: Collocation: full with/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: Collocation: in/at a party
rule.message: Unless you mean "in a political party", the usual preposition for a party is "at". at a party

rule.name: Wrong phrase: 'in nowadays' (nowadays)
rule.message: \2 is used without 'in'. \2

rule.name: In the recent years (in recent years)
rule.message: The definite article "the" is not necessary in this context. \1 \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: motivation in/to
rule.message: Do not use "motivation in \3". Use "to" + infinitive form instead. motivation to \3

rule.name: Collocation: near from/to
rule.message: The usual preposition for "near" is "to". near to

rule.name: Collocation: perspectives about/on
rule.message: The usual preposition for "perspectives" is "on", not "about". perspectives on

rule.name: Collocation: tired about/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of" or "from", not "\2". Did you mean tired of or tired from?

rule.name: Wrong phrase: 'today night' (tonight)
rule.message: This is a non-standard collocation. Normally, tonight is used instead.

rule.name: Collocation: varieties in/of
rule.message: The usual preposition for "varieties" is "of", not "in". varieties of

rule.name: Collocation: vulnerable from/to
rule.message: The usual collocation for the word "vulnerable" is "to", not "from", as in "vulnerable to attack". vulnerable to

rule.name: Wrong phrase: 'yesterday night' (last night)
rule.message: This is a non-standard collocation. Normally, last night is used.

rule.name: Collocation: absorb at/in
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "in", not "\2". Did you mean \1 in?

rule.name: Collocation: accompany with/by
rule.message: The usual collocation for "accompany" is "by", not "with". \1 by

rule.name: Collocation: accuse for/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: Collocation: accustom with/to
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "to", not "\2". Did you mean \1 to?

rule.name: Collocation: Admitted at/to
rule.message: The usual collocation for "admit" is "to", not "at". \1 to \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: aim on/at
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "at", not "\2". Did you mean \1 at?

rule.name: Collocation: angry with/about
rule.message: Use "angry with" for people. Use "angry about" for situations or weather. Did you mean \1 about the \4?

rule.name: Collocation: anxious of/about
rule.message: The usual preposition with "\1" is "about" or "for". Did you mean anxious about or anxious for?

rule.name: Collocation: apply for/to
rule.message: You can apply "for" a job or welfare, but you apply "to" a \4. \1 to \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: apply to/for
rule.message: You can apply "to" a company or university, but you apply "for" \4. \1 for \3 \4 \5

rule.name: Collocation: arrived (in the) downtown
rule.message: Did you mean simply \1 \4? You do not need the word "in" here.

rule.name: Collocation: arrive at/in
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "in", not "\2". Use "in" for geographical regions and "at" for a specific point. For a town, province, state, country or continent, use "in". For a restaurant, business, park or car, use "at". Did you mean \1 in or '\1 at'?

rule.name: Collocation: at/in the morning
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\3" is "in". in the \3

rule.name: Collocation: Between ... to/and ...
rule.message: After "between", use "and", not "to". \1 \2 and \4

rule.name: Collocation: be in/under pressure
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\3" is "under", not "\2". Did you mean under pressure?

rule.name: Collocation: be on/in shock
rule.message: The usual preposition with "\3" is "in". Did you mean \1 in shock?

rule.name: Collocation: boast for/about/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of" or "about", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of or \1 about?

rule.name: Collocation: bring at/to
rule.message: The usual preposition for "bring" is "to". Did you mean \1 \2 to?

rule.name: Collocation: bring to/put on the agenda
rule.message: The usual verb + preposition for "agenda" are "put on". Did you mean put on the agenda?

rule.name: bring SOMEBODY in/to
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "to", not "\3". Did you mean \1 \2 to \4 \5?

rule.name: Translation errors: by/for example
rule.message: The best translation for the introductory phrase "\1 \2" is "for example". Did you mean For example?

rule.name: Collocation: come in/into
rule.message: The words "come in" and "come to" have different meanings. "Come in \3 office" means "have an orgasm in \3 office". "Come to" or "Come into" means "enter". come to

rule.name: came to/by
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2" is "by". \1 \2 by \4

rule.name: Collocation: complain for/about
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "about", not "\2". Did you mean \1 about?

rule.name: Collocation: compose from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: Collocation: Consist to/of
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1" is "of", not "to". \1 of

rule.name: Collocation: cover by/in
rule.message: The usual preposition for "cover" is "in", not "by". covered in \3

rule.name: I crave for () chocolate
rule.message: If you are using "\1" as a verb, then you do not need the preposition "for" here.

rule.name: Collocation: Crazy on/with
rule.message: The usual preposition for "crazy" is "with", not "on". crazy with

rule.name: Collocation: cure against/for
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "for", not "against". Did you mean \1 for?

rule.name: Collocation: cure from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 \2 of?

rule.name: Collocation: deprive from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\3". Did you mean \1 \2 of?

rule.name: made (did) a mistake
rule.message: Did you mean made?

rule.name: Collocation: difficult to/for
rule.message: The usual preposition after "difficult" is "for" when followed by a pronoun. "Difficult" and "to" only appear together when "to" is part of an infinitive structure. \1 for \3

rule.name: Collocation: disappoint from/with
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "with" or "in" or "by" or "at" or "about", not "\2". Did you mean \1 with or \1 in or \1 by or \1 at or \1 about?

rule.name: Collocation: a diversity of colors OR diverse colors
rule.message: The word "of" is missing between "\2" and "\3". \2 of \3

rule.name: Collocation: do/have a meeting
rule.message: The usual verb for "a meeting" is "have", not "do" \1 a meeting

rule.name: Collocation: dress with/in
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\3" is "in", not "\4". Did you mean \3 in?

rule.name: eat (take) antibiotics
rule.message: In a medical context, people take or swallow \2. If they eat \2, it is a mistake. Did you mean \1 or \1?

rule.name: I enrolled international school
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2" is "in" (a school) or "for" (a course). Did you mean \1 \2 in or \1 \2 for?

rule.name: Collocation: enter in
rule.message: There isn't usually a preposition after "\1". Did you mean \1 \3 \4 \5?

rule.name: Collocation: follow/take a course
rule.message: The usual verb for "courses" in a school is "take" or "attend", not "follow". take \2 courses

rule.name: Translation error: a gesture of greeting
rule.message: This expression sounds awkward. Did you mean waved at me?

rule.name: Collocation: get a job in/with
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1 \2 \3" is "with" for an organization. with \5 \6

rule.name: Collocation: glad with/about
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "about", not "\2". Did you mean \1 about?

rule.name: going on vacation
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\3" is "on". \1 on \3

rule.name: Collocation: good in/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for this expression is "at". \1 at \3

rule.name: Collocation: go to home (go home)
rule.message: There isn't usually a preposition before "\3". Did you mean \1 \3?

rule.name: Collocation: growled to/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1" is "at". \1 at \3

rule.name: Wrong phrase: have x years old (be x years old)
rule.message: This phrase is used with 'be'. \1 \2 \3 \4

rule.name: Wrong phrase: I have x years old (I am x years old)
rule.message: This phrase is used with 'be'. am \3 \4 \5

rule.name: Collocation: imitation from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for the word "imitate" is "of", not "from", as in "imitate the style of someone". \1 \2 \3 of \5 \6

rule.name: Collocation: Important with/to
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1" is "to", not "with". \1 to \3

rule.name: Collocation: insist on + gerund
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "on", not "\2". Did you mean \1 on \3?

rule.name: Collocation: interested by/in
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "in", not "by". \1 in

rule.name: Collocation: interest about/in
rule.message: The usual collocation for the word "interest" is "in", not "about". \1 \2 in

rule.name: Collocation: invoice of/for
rule.message: The usual preposition to use after "\1 \2" is "for", not "of". Did you mean \1 \2 for?

rule.name: Collocation: at/in the morning
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\3" is "in", not "at". in the \3

rule.name: Collocation: in/at the top
rule.message: The usual preposition to use with "bottom" or "top" is "at", not "in". Did you mean at \2 \3?

rule.name: Collocation: in charge of/for
rule.message: The usual preposition to use after "\1 \2" is "for", not "of". Did you mean \1 \2 for?

rule.name: in (on) Facebook
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2" is "on". Did you mean on \2?

rule.name: Collocation: in/from the perspective
rule.message: The usual preposition for "perspective" is "from", not "in". from \2 \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: in/on a secret mission
rule.message: The usual preposition for "a secret mission" is "on", not "in". Try it like this: \1 on \3 \4 \5.

rule.name: Collocation: in/on a team
rule.message: The usual preposition for "team" is "on", not "in". on \5 \6

rule.name: Collocation: in/on a trip
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2 \3" is on \2 \3 or during \2 \3.

rule.name: Collocation: in/on the right hand side
rule.message: In this case, the usual preposition with "side" is "on", not "in". Did you mean on \2 \3 \4 \5?

rule.name: Collocation: in/on the Internet
rule.message: The usual collocation for technology is "on", not "in". on the \3

rule.name: Collocation: in/on the outside
rule.message: The usual preposition for "outside" is "on", not "in". on the outside

rule.name: Collocation: in/at this moment
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2 \3" is "at", not "\1". Did you mean at \2 \3?

rule.name: Collocation: it is sure/certain
rule.message: "It is sure" is uncommon. Consider using certain.

rule.name: Collocation: kind with/to
rule.message: The usual preposition to use with "\1" is "to". \1 to

rule.name: Collocation: laugh of/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for "laugh" is "at". Did you mean \1 at \3?

rule.name: Collocation: leave to/for
rule.message: The usual preposition for "leave" is "for", not "to". leaving for \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: live in/on planet
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\4" is "on". Did you mean \1 on \3 \4?

rule.name: Collocation: located on (at) Main street
rule.message: The usual preposition for an exact address is "at", not "on". Did you mean located at \3 \4 \5?

rule.name: Collocation: Look at/to
rule.message: The usual preposition for "mirror" is "in", not "at". \1 in a mirror

rule.name: Collocation: lot (of)
rule.message: If you mean a great quantity, use \1 \2 of \3.

rule.name: Collocation: lunch to/for
rule.message: The usual preposition to use when you do something for the benefit of another person is "for". Did you mean \1 \2 for \4 \5?

rule.name: Missing preposition
rule.message: It appears that a preposition is missing after '\2'. \2 to \3

rule.name: He knocked (on) the door
rule.message: It looks like you're missing the preposition "on". Did you mean \1 on?

rule.name: Wrong preposition: 'obvious for' (obvious to)
rule.message: The adjective '\1' is normally used with 'to'. \1 to \3

rule.name: Collocation: offered in/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for "offered" and "\3" is "at", not "in". offered at \3

rule.name: Collocation: on/in fact
rule.message: The usual preposition for "fact" is "in". Did you mean In fact?

rule.name: Collocation: on/in a hospital bed
rule.message: The usual preposition with "\5" is "in". Did you mean in a hospital bed?

rule.name: Collocation: on/in charge of
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\5" is "in". Did you mean \1 \2 \3 in charge of?

rule.name: Collocation: on/in the corner
rule.message: The usual preposition to use with "corner" is "in", not "on". Did you mean in \2 \3 \4 \5?

rule.name: Collocation: on/in the corner
rule.message: The usual preposition to use with "corner" is "in", not "on". Did you mean in \2 \3 \4?

rule.name: open at (to) page 6
rule.message: Did you mean to?

rule.name: Collocation: Opposite from/to
rule.message: Use "to" with "opposite". \1 to \3

rule.name: Collocation: Participate to/in
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\1" is "in", not "to". \1 in

rule.name: Collocation: Passionate by/about
rule.message: The usual preposition for "passionate" is "about", not "by". passionate about

rule.name: Collocation: Play with/for
rule.message: Use "for" with "play". \1 \2 for

rule.name: Collocation: Prefer instead of/to
rule.message: Consider using "to" with "prefer". \1 \2 \3 to \6 \7

rule.name: Collocation: Prefer over/to
rule.message: Consider using "to" with "prefer". \1 \2 \3 to \5 \6

rule.name: Collocation: Repercussion with/on
rule.message: The usual preposition for "repercussion" is "on", not "with". \1 on

rule.name: Respectful against/toward
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "toward" but never "\2". Did you mean \1 toward?

rule.name: Collocation: responsible of/for
rule.message: The usual collocation for "responsible" is "responsible for". Did you mean responsible for or in charge of?

rule.name: Collocation: return at home
rule.message: You do not need a preposition between "\1" and "\3". Did you mean \1 \3?

rule.name: Collocation: rid from/of
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "of", not "\2". Did you mean \1 of?

rule.name: a sinking (s)hip
rule.message: Did you mean ship?

rule.name: Collocation: sit on the court/bench
rule.message: The usual way to express a judge's association with a courtroom is with the phrase \1 on the bench.

rule.name: Collocation: spend it for/on
rule.message: The usual prepositions that follow "spend it" are either "on" or "in". Did you mean spend it on?

rule.name: steps to do/take
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "take". Did you mean \1 \2 take?

rule.name: Collocation: Stress out for/over
rule.message: The usual preposition in this context is "over" or "about". \1 \2 over \4

rule.name: Collocation: suffer of/with/from
rule.message: The usual preposition for this expression is "from" or "with". \1 from

rule.name: Collocation: Summon at/to
rule.message: The usual collocation is "summon to" a place, or "summon at" a time/moment. Use "to" instead: \1 to \3 \4 \5 \6.

rule.name: Collocation: takes into consideration (of)
rule.message: You have fused two expressions into one, "in consideration of" and "taking into consideration". Remove the word "of" from the end. \1 \2 \3

rule.name: Collocation: talk down about/to
rule.message: The usual preposition for this expression is "to". Did you mean \1 down to?

rule.name: 'Thank' + Proper noun
rule.message: Did you mean \2s to \3 or \2s \3,?

rule.name: 'Thank the' + noun
rule.message: Did you mean \2s to or \2s for?

rule.name: work (for) is
rule.message: The word "for" is missing between "\5" and "\6". \1 \2 that \4 \5 for \6

rule.name: Collocation: think on/about
rule.message: The usual collocations for "\1" are \1 about or \1 of.

rule.name: Collocation: Tired of/from
rule.message: The usual preposition in this context is "from". tired from last night

rule.name: Wrong phrase: 'today morning' (this morning)
rule.message: This is a non-standard collocation. Normally, this \2 is used.

rule.name: Collocation: to found/find a cure
rule.message: The verb "to found" means "to establish", whereas "to find" means "to discover". to find \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: to/on a trip
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\2" and "a trip" is "\2 on a trip". Did you mean \1 on a trip?

rule.name: To whom it may concern
rule.message: Did you mean the introductory phrase to whom it may concern?

rule.name: travel (for) X days
rule.message: The word "for" is missing between "\2" and "\3". \1 traveled for \3 \4

rule.name: Collocation: travel with/by
rule.message: The usual collocation for "\1" is "by", not "\2". Did you mean \1 by \4?

rule.name: Collocation: Sit in/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for activities involving a desk is "at". \1 \2 at \4 \5

rule.name: Collocation: waiting (to) finish
rule.message: The word "to" is missing before the infinitive form "\4". \1 \2 \3 to \4

rule.name: Collocation: waiting after (for) someone
rule.message: The usual collocations for "\1" are "\1 on", meaning 'to act as an attendant to someone', or "\1 for" meaning 'to remain patiently inactive'. \1 for \3

rule.name: Collocation: Well in/on
rule.message: Use "\2 on" with "\4". Try this: \1 \2 on \4.

rule.name: Collocation: we call/you would call
rule.message: This expression sounds awkward. Did you mean \2 \3 you would call?

rule.name: Collocation: in (on) university projects
rule.message: The usual preposition for "\3...projects" is "on". Did you mean \3 \4 \5 \6 on \8 projects?

rule.name: Wrong collocation: 'worry for' (worry about)
rule.message: The verb 'worry' does not normally take the preposition 'for'. Did you mean \1 about?

rule.name: Collocation: yell on/at
rule.message: The usual preposition for "yell" is "at". \1 at \3

rule.name: admin (admit)
rule.message: Did you mean admit (=confess)?

rule.name: Adapt to v. adopt to
rule.message: Did you mean adapt to?

rule.name: 'all the further' is a common, but incorrect phrase
rule.message: Did you mean as far as?

rule.name: Amendable to error
rule.message: Did you mean amenable?

rule.name: Ascetic acid v acetic acid
rule.message: Did you mean acetic acid?

rule.name: Missing 'as' in 'as well as or better than'
rule.message: Did you mean as well as or better than?

rule.name: Awaiting for - incorrect
rule.message: Did you mean awaiting or waiting for?

rule.name: once a (or) twice
rule.message: Did you mean or?

rule.name: back and fourth (back and forth)
rule.message: Did you mean back and forth?

rule.name: back in forth (back and forth)
rule.message: Did you mean back and forth?

rule.name: brake away (break away)
rule.message: Did you mean break away?

rule.name: bye the way (by the way)
rule.message: Did you mean by the way?

rule.name: calender (calendar)
rule.message: Did you mean calendar, a system of organizing days? A 'calender' is a machine.

rule.name: chalk full (chock-full)
rule.message: Did you mean chock-full?

rule.name: It's an allusion (illusion) of not being lonely
rule.message: In most contexts, you allude "to" something. Did you mean to write "illusion" here? illusion of

rule.name: another (in other)
rule.message: Did you mean in other words?

rule.name: sobber (sober)
rule.message: Did you mean sober (=not intoxicated)? "Sobber" means "weeper, someone one who cries".

rule.name: confusion of yet alone → let alone
rule.message: Did you mean let alone?

rule.name: deep-seeded (deep-seated)
rule.message: Did you mean deep-seated?

rule.name: Dependent
rule.message: Did you mean dependent on?

rule.name: dod vs did
rule.message: Did you mean did \2?

rule.name: doggy-dog (dog-eat-dog)
rule.message: Did you mean dog-eat-dog?

rule.name: 'Do to that fact that' → 'Due to'
rule.message: Use due to or because of to fix spelling and readability of this sentence.

rule.name: ease off (of) use
rule.message: Did you mean of?

rule.name: egg yoke (egg yolk)
rule.message: Did you mean egg yolk?

rule.name: en mass (en masse)
rule.message: Did you mean en masse?

rule.name: et all (et al.)
rule.message: Did you mean et al. (=and others) or at all?

rule.name: formally known as (formerly known as)
rule.message: Did you mean formerly \2 \3 (=known earlier under the name of)?

rule.name: for sell (for sale)
rule.message: Did you mean for sale?

rule.name: from than (then) on
rule.message: Did you mean from then on?

rule.name: Further ado instead of further adieu
rule.message: Did you mean further ado?

rule.name: happed vs happened
rule.message: Did you mean happened or happen?

rule.name: insure that (ensure that)
rule.message: Did you mean ensure (=make sure)? 'Insure' means 'pay money to insurance company'.

rule.name: intensive purposes (intents and purposes)
rule.message: Did you mean intents and \3?

rule.name: in masse (en masse)
rule.message: Did you mean en masse?

rule.name: in the passed (in the past)
rule.message: Did you mean in the past?

rule.name: a blessing in the skies (disguise)
rule.message: Did you mean disguise (a blessing in disguise = an unfortunate event that results in a positive outcome)?

rule.name: in vein (in vain)
rule.message: Did you mean in vain (=unsuccessfully)?

rule.name: Japanise vs Japanese
rule.message: Did you mean Japanese (= people from Japan)?

rule.name: Mabey vs. Maybe
rule.message: "Mabey" is a common surname. Did you mean maybe (= perhaps)?

rule.name: marriage (wedding) anniversary
rule.message: Wedding anniversaries are the proper term to describe the anniversary of the day of a wedding. wedding

rule.name: one foul (fell) swoop
rule.message: Did you mean fell?

rule.name: on behave (on behalf)
rule.message: Did you mean on behalf (in the name of, as a representative of)?

rule.name: opining vs. opening
rule.message: Did you mean opening (= present participle of "to open")?

rule.name: price sensibility (sensitivity)
rule.message: Did you mean price sensitivity (= degree to which the price of a product affects consumers' purchasing behaviors)?

rule.name: sigh vs sight
rule.message: Did you mean the noun sight?

rule.name: sneak peak (sneak peek)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 peek (=preview)?

rule.name: summery vs summary
rule.message: "\1" (= fit for summer) is an adjective. Did you mean summary (= abstract)?

rule.name: ..., than, ... (then)
rule.message: Did you mean then?

rule.name: Aide in wrong context
rule.message: Did you mean to aid?

rule.name: who than (then)
rule.message: Did you mean then?

rule.name: wildly (widely) accepted
rule.message: Did you mean widely?

rule.name: wining vs winning
rule.message: Did you mean winning (= to win the championship) or whining (= to cry)?

rule.name: witch haunt (witch hunt)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 hunt (=search for witches)?

rule.name: zero-sum gain (game)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 game?

rule.name: addend vs attend
rule.message: "Addend" is a noun. Did you mean the verb attend (= to participate in an event)?

rule.name: airplane hanger (airplane hangar)
rule.message: Did you mean \2?

rule.name: 'ally' used in wrong situation
rule.message: Did you mean allay?

rule.name: amassing/amazing
rule.message: Did you mean the adjective amazing (= great)?

rule.name: and than (then)
rule.message: Did you mean then?

rule.name: anus (angus)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 (verb) instead of \2 (noun)?

rule.name: a priory (a priori)
rule.message: Did you mean the Latin term a priori?

rule.name: Architect as a verb
rule.message: Architect is a noun, not a verb. Did you mean design or devise?
rule.short: Architect is a noun, not a verb

rule.name: are vs our
rule.message: Did you mean our?

rule.name: ask wether/weather (whether)
rule.message: Did you mean whether?

rule.name: Ass vs As
rule.message: Did you mean as?

rule.name: as vs. us
rule.message: Did you mean us?

rule.name: Axed v asked
rule.message: Did you mean asked?

rule.name: In a hastily way
rule.message: Did you mean hasty \4?

rule.name: taken back (aback) by
rule.message: Did you mean 'taken aback' (=surprised or shocked)? aback

rule.name: bar-b-queue instead of 'bar-b-que'
rule.message: Did you mean barbeque or \1?

rule.name: have bean (been)
rule.message: Did you mean the verb been?

rule.name: bee vs be
rule.message: Did you mean be?

rule.name: begone (begun)
rule.message: Did you mean begun (= past participle of 'begin')?

rule.name: belief (believe)
rule.message: Did you mean \3 (verb) instead of \3 (noun)?

rule.name: aloud to (allowed to)
rule.message: Possible typo. Did you mean allowed to?

rule.name: bis vs bus
rule.message: Did you mean the noun bus?

rule.name: bob/barb wire (barbed wire)
rule.message: Did you mean barbed wire?

rule.name: a bold-faced (bald-faced) lie
rule.message: Did you mean bald-faced (= showing no shame)?

rule.name: buy (by) + gerund verb
rule.message: Did you mean by?

rule.name: carries vs caries
rule.message: Did you mean the verb carries ("to carry")?

rule.name: coincident vs coincidence
rule.message: Did you mean the noun coincidence?

rule.name: ..., than (then)
rule.message: Did you mean then?

rule.name: await (wait)
rule.message: Did you mean \1?

rule.name: awl (all)
rule.message: Did you mean all \2 \3?

rule.name: knot (not)
rule.message: Did you mean the negation \1 not?

rule.name: addicting (addictive)
rule.message: Did you mean the adjective addictive instead of the verb form 'addicting'?

rule.name: corral (coral)
rule.message: Did you mean coral?

rule.name: hard (heart)
rule.message: Did you mean heart?

rule.name: marshal/marital (martial)
rule.message: Did you mean martial (relating to war or a warrior)?

rule.name: corse vs course
rule.message: Did you mean course?

rule.name: counsel (consul) general
rule.message: Did you mean consul \2?

rule.name: decease (disease)
rule.message: Did you mean disease (=sickness)?

rule.name: diffuse (defuse) bomb
rule.message: Did you mean \1 (=removing the fuse from a bomb)? "Diffuse" as a verb means to spread out.

rule.name: deus ex machina
rule.message: The correct spelling for this expression is deus ex machina.

rule.name: de jure (du jour)
rule.message: '\1 \2' is a legal term. Did you mean du jour or more simply of the day?

rule.name: diffuse (defuse) tensions
rule.message: Did you mean \1, as in removing the fuse from a bomb? "Diffuse" as a verb means to spread out.

rule.name: 'Do to the lack of' → 'Due to lack of'
rule.message: Did you mean due to the \4 of?

rule.name: dun vs don't
rule.message: Did you mean don't?

rule.name: 'economical (economic) growth' etc.
rule.message: Did you mean economic (=connected with economy)?

rule.name: 'economic (economical) car' etc.
rule.message: Did you mean economical (=affordable, cheap)?

rule.name: effort vs afford
rule.message: The word "\4" is a noun. Did you mean afford?

rule.name: fare vs fair
rule.message: Did you mean the adjective fair?

rule.name: fee vs free
rule.message: Did you mean free?

rule.name: first aid kid (kit)
rule.message: Did you mean kit (=set of articles, tools, or supplies) instead of 'kid' (=young person)?

rule.name: fir vs fit
rule.message: Did you mean the verb fit?

rule.name: fir vs for
rule.message: Did you mean the preposition for?

rule.name: flee (flea) market
rule.message: Did you mean flea \2 (= street market)?

rule.name: fore vs for
rule.message: Did you mean for or force?

rule.name: Looks good form (for) me
rule.message: Did you mean for?

rule.name: forward vs. foreword
rule.message: Did you mean the adverb forward?

rule.name: for all intensive purposes (for all intents and purposes)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 all intents and purposes?

rule.name: foud vs found
rule.message: Did you mean the verb found (= past tense of "to find")?

rule.name: functionally vs functionality
rule.message: Did you mean the noun functionality?

rule.name: genera vs general
rule.message: "Genera" is the plural noun for "genus". Did you mean the adjective general?

rule.name: grand vs grant
rule.message: Did you mean the verb grant? "Grand" is an adjective.

rule.name: take for granite (granted)
rule.message: Did you mean granted?

rule.name: gues vs guess
rule.message: Did you mean the verb guess?

rule.name: had vs. hard
rule.message: Did you mean hard?

rule.name: He has a hart (heart) of a lion
rule.message: Did you mean to write 'heart' here? heart

rule.name: hears vs years
rule.message: Did you mean years?

rule.name: her vs hear
rule.message: Did you mean the verb hear?

rule.name: he vs be
rule.message: Did you mean be?

rule.name: hwy vs why
rule.message: "Hwy" is the abbreviation for "Highway". Did you mean why?

rule.name: id vs is
rule.message: Did you mean the verb is?

rule.name: ind vs kind
rule.message: Did you mean kind?

rule.name: in (on) vacation
rule.message: Did you mean on vacation?

rule.name: you're (your)
rule.message: Did you mean your?

rule.name: it vs si
rule.message: Did you mean is?

rule.name: I as (was) looking
rule.message: Did you mean was?

rule.name: I herd (heard)
rule.message: Did you mean heard (past tense of 'hear')?

rule.name: I wander (wonder) if
rule.message: Did you mean wonder?

rule.name: key stoke (stroke)
rule.message: Did you mean \2?

rule.name: knew (new)
rule.message: Did you mean new?

rule.name: lear vs learn
rule.message: Did you mean the verb learn?

rule.name: leas vs least
rule.message: Did you mean least?

rule.name: lending vs. landing
rule.message: Did you mean landing \2?

rule.name: less/more ... then (than)
rule.message: Did you mean than?

rule.name: let is (us) know
rule.message: Did you mean us or it?

rule.name: Let me trough (through)!
rule.message: Did you mean through?

rule.name: loot vs lot
rule.message: Did you mean \1 \2?

rule.name: mange vs manage
rule.message: Did you mean \4?

rule.name: manner vs matter
rule.message: Did you mean manner (= in a way)?

rule.name: manor vs manner
rule.message: Did you mean manner (= a way in which a thing is done or happens)?

rule.name: marry (merry)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 (=jolly, festive)?

rule.name: Mary (Merry) Christmas
rule.message: Did you mean the Christmas greeting merry Christmas?

rule.name: mater vs matter
rule.message: Did you mean matter?

rule.name: ma vs my
rule.message: Did you mean my?

rule.name: mean vs meant
rule.message: Did you mean meant?

rule.name: meed (meet)
rule.message: Did you mean \1?

rule.name: me vs be
rule.message: Did you mean be?

rule.name: minuet (minute)
rule.message: Did you mean \1?

rule.name: mis vs miss
rule.message: Did you mean the verb miss?

rule.name: Moist vs. Most
rule.message: Did you mean most?

rule.name: more vs move
rule.message: Did you mean the verb move?

rule.name: mush vs. much
rule.message: Did you mean much?

rule.name: naive vs native
rule.message: Did you mean native?

rule.name: niece (nice) day etc.
rule.message: Did you mean nice (=pleasant)?

rule.name: nit vs not
rule.message: Did you mean not?

rule.name: not jet (yet)
rule.message: Did you mean yet?

rule.name: oedometer (odometer)
rule.message: An oedometer is an instrument used in geotechnical investigation. Did you mean \1 (an instrument that measures the distance that a vehicle travels)?

rule.name: opinion/... pole (poll)
rule.message: Did you mean poll?

rule.name: our vs your
rule.message: Did you mean your?

rule.name: overdue vs overdo
rule.message: Did you mean the verb overdo?

rule.name: overnight instead of 'over night'
rule.message: Did you mean the verb, adjective or adverb \1\2 (i.e., for or during the entire night)?

rule.name: o (to)
rule.message: Did you mean to?

rule.name: a pare (pair) of
rule.message: The word "\3" is a verb. Did you mean the noun pair?

rule.name: to walk passed (past)
rule.message: Did you mean past? 'passed' is a form of the verb 'pass'.

rule.name: peddle (paddle/pedal)
rule.message: Did you mean paddle\2 (= used to move a boat) or pedal\2 (= used to ride or drive)?

rule.name: per vs pre
rule.message: Did you mean per?

rule.name: pigeon (pidgin) English/...
rule.message: Did you mean pidgin (=simplified language)?

rule.name: rater vs rather
rule.message: Did you mean the adverb rather?

rule.name: ration or ratio
rule.message: Did you mean ratio?

rule.name: relieve vs relief
rule.message: It appears the correct noun in this context is relief.

rule.name: road vs rode
rule.message: The past tense of the verb "to ride" is rode.

rule.name: roll vs role
rule.message: Did you mean the verb \1?

rule.name: route (root) of the problem
rule.message: Did you mean \1 (=source, cause)?

rule.name: se vs see
rule.message: Did you mean See?

rule.name: sigh vs sign
rule.message: Did you mean the verb \1?

rule.name: slight (sleight) of hand
rule.message: Did you mean \1?

rule.name: sometime vs sometimes
rule.message: It seems that sometimes fits better in this context. Please check.

rule.name: state of art (state of the art, state-of-the-art)
rule.message: The phrase '\1 \2 \3' is correct if it refers to the condition of art. But, if you mean 'most recent technological development', use the noun \1 \2 the \3 or the adjective state-of-the-art.

rule.name: striped (stripped)
rule.message: Did you mean stripped (= without clothes / deprived of)?

rule.name: stripped (striped)
rule.message: Did you mean striped (= having stripes)?

rule.name: tail vs tale
rule.message: Did you mean \2?

rule.name: take case (care) of it
rule.message: Did you mean care?

rule.name: I am more tan (than) pleased
rule.message: Did you mean to write "than" here? than

rule.name: football teem (football team)
rule.message: Did you mean \1 team?

rule.name: telephone/... poll (pole)
rule.message: Did you mean pole?

rule.name: That being sad (said)
rule.message: Did you mean That being said?

rule.name: where (were)
rule.message: Did you mean were, the simple past of "to be"?

rule.name: the flew (flu)
rule.message: Did you mean flu (flew is past tense of fly, flu is a viral disease)?

rule.name: a through(thorough) discussion etc.
rule.message: Did you mean thorough (accurate, exhaustive)?

rule.name: too vs tool
rule.message: Did you mean two or tool?

rule.name: torcher (torture) yourself
rule.message: Did you mean the verb \3 (= punish, to cause suffering)?

rule.name: tor vs to
rule.message: Did you mean to?

rule.name: true vs truth
rule.message: Did you mean the noun truth?

rule.name: Unites (United)
rule.message: Did you mean United \2?

rule.name: The decision sometimes various (varies)
rule.message: Did you mean to write the verb "varies" here? varies

rule.name: 'written in verses (verse)'
rule.message: Did you mean verse?

rule.name: I was wandering (wondering) if
rule.message: Did you mean wondering?

rule.name: we vs wee
rule.message: Did you mean we?

rule.name: were vs wear
rule.message: Did you mean the verb wear (= "to wear a dress")?

rule.name: we'll vs well
rule.message: Did you mean well?

rule.name: what it (is) happening
rule.message: Did you mean is?

rule.name: what it (is) the
rule.message: Did you mean is?

rule.name: which vs wish
rule.message: Did you mean wish?

rule.name: whit vs with
rule.message: Did you mean with (whit = a tiny amount)?

rule.name: witch (which) is wrong
rule.message: Did you mean which?

rule.name: won vs own
rule.message: Did you mean own?

rule.name: wood vs would
rule.message: Did you mean the modal verb would?

rule.name: works as (like) a charm

rule.name: the proof is in the pudding (the proof of the pudding is in the eating)
rule.message: If you don't want to look for proofs hidden in your pudding, you probably meant the proof of the pudding is in the eating.

rule.name: very known (very well-known, well-known)
rule.message: Incorrect phrase. Use very well-known or well-known instead.

rule.name: from X - Y (from X to Y)
rule.message: Using a hyphen or dash in a “from … to …” expression is incorrect. Did you mean: \1 \2 to \4?
rule.short: Using a hyphen or dash in a “from … to …” expression is incorrect.

rule.name: in/with regards to/of (regarding, with regard to)
rule.message: Although "\1 \2 \3" is sometimes used in casual speech, it is typically considered a nonstandard phrase. in regard to

rule.name: redundant determiner: 'in (an) alphabetical order
rule.message: Unless there is more than one '\3 \4', leave out the determiner '\2'. \1 \3 \4

rule.name: I never have been (I have never been)
rule.message: Although not incorrect, the word order have never is preferred by native speakers.

rule.name: February of 2005 (February 2005)
rule.message: When specifying a month and year, 'of' is unnecessary. \1 \3

rule.name: 5-farads/5-farad
rule.message: The word '\1' is an adjective (SI unit of measurement), but the spelling is not correct. Do not use a plural adjective. Refer to section 5.4.3 in 'SI Brochure: The International System of Units (SI)'.

rule.name: the question what (the question of what)
rule.message: This sounds awkward in English. The standard is \1 \2 of \3.

rule.name: whip cream (whipped cream)
rule.message: Did you mean whipped cream?

rule.name: anymore (any more)
rule.message: In British English, the spelling '\1' is sometimes considered incorrect. Did you mean any more?

rule.name: gotten/got
rule.message: "Gotten" is commonly used in American English. For varieties outside of North America, "got" is the preferred variant. got

rule.name: ground beef/minced beef
rule.message: The term '\1 \2' is common for American English. Did you mean minced \2?

rule.name: air plane/aeroplane
rule.message: The term '\1 \2' is common for American English. For British English use \2.

rule.name: driver's licence/driving licence
rule.message: This term is chiefly North American English. Consider a replacement. driving \4

rule.name: eggplant/aubergine
rule.message: This term is chiefly North American English. Consider a replacement. \1

rule.name: movie theater/cinema
rule.message: The term '\1 \2' is common for American English. Did you mean \2?

rule.name: ok (OK)
rule.message: Lower-case '\1' is American English. For British English use OK.

rule.name: 1-liter/1-litre
rule.message: The word '\1' is an adjective (SI unit of measurement), but the spelling is not correct British English.

rule.name: trunk/boot (of car)
rule.message: In the context of cars (automobiles), '\2' is American English. For British English use \2.

rule.name: zip code/postcode
rule.message: The term '\1 \2' is common for American English. Did you mean \2?

rule.name: anyways (anyway)
rule.message: The word '\1' is informal American English. Did you mean anyway?

rule.name: an invite (invitation)
rule.message: The noun invitation is usually used instead of 'invite' in formal writing.

rule.name: bestest (best)
rule.message: The word "\1" is informal. best

rule.name: bored of (with)
rule.message: In standard English it's more accepted to use bored with or bored by.

rule.name: busses (buses)
rule.message: The more common plural form of "bus" (= motor vehicle) is buses.

rule.name: Smart ellipsis (…)
rule.message: Consider using the typographical ellipsis character here instead. …

rule.name: fastly (quickly)
rule.message: The adverb "fastly" is considered archaic. Consider an alternative. quickly

rule.name: for ever (forever)
rule.message: It is more common to spell this adverb as one word. forever

rule.name: irregardless (regardless)
rule.message: 'Irregardless' is nonstandard. In formal style you should use regardless.

rule.name: lemme (let me)
rule.message: The word '\1' is informal. let me

rule.name: 'ourself'
rule.message: The term is non-standard and not widely accepted. ourselves

rule.name: outta (out of)
rule.message: The word '\1' is informal. out of

rule.name: Thank you much
rule.message: This phrase is non-standard. Consider replacing it. \1 \2 so \3

rule.name: 'themself'
rule.message: Generally speaking, "themself" is only acceptable when referring to a singular entity (such as the singular usage of "they", which is the preferred pronoun for many non-binary people). If "themself" refers to a plural entity (such as "everybody", or the standard usage of "they"), you should use "themselves". themselves

rule.name: Tiflis
rule.message: The capital city of Georgia is today known under the name Tbilisi.

rule.name: used to could (be able to)
rule.message: Did you mean be able to?

rule.name: to be able to + 'passive voice'
rule.message: Avoid the passive voice after 'to be able to'.

rule.name: dat (that)
rule.message: Did you mean that or date?

rule.name: different than (from)
rule.message: Did you mean 'different from'? 'Different than' is often considered colloquial style. from

rule.name: dis (this)
rule.message: Did you mean this?

rule.name: Readability: Four nouns in a row
rule.message: Using four (or more) nouns in a row may decrease readability.

rule.name: gimme (give me)
rule.message: The word "\1" is informal. give me

rule.name: gotcha (got you)
rule.message: The word "\1" is informal. got you

rule.name: luv (love)
rule.message: The word '\1' is informal and non-standard. \1

rule.name: 'merchandize' vs. 'merchandise'
rule.message: Consider using the more commonly used \1.

rule.name: most (almost) every
rule.message: Avoid the colloquial phrase '\1 \2' in formal writing. Use almost \2 instead.

rule.name: Most of our times (time)
rule.message: Did you mean the more common phrase \2 \3 \4 \5?

rule.name: 'Needs fixed' type construction
rule.message: The double modal "\1 \2" is nonstandard (only accepted in certain dialects). Consider to be \2.

rule.name: transitive verb 'occasion' is very formal
rule.message: The transitive verb "\1" is a very formal word choice. Consider a more common synonym. \1

rule.name: Style: 'possible' after 'possibility'
rule.message: Before '\2', use a word such as \1 or \1. Alternatively, keep '\1' and replace '\2'.

rule.name: abbreviation 'ppl'
rule.message: The abbreviation for people can be considered informal.

rule.name: It's supposed to prevent it (from) spilling over
rule.message: Try inserting 'from' here. from \3

rule.name: be bigger than me (I)
rule.message: In formal / written English, it is recommended that you use a subject pronoun. I

rule.name: there're → there are
rule.message: The contraction '\1\2' is uncommon in written English. \1 are

rule.name: I don't wanna to travel (want to travel)
rule.message: The word 'wanna' is informal, consider replacing it. want \2 \3

rule.name: week-end (weekend)
rule.message: This word might seem dated. Consider spelling it without a hyphen. \1

rule.name: a whole other (entirely different) issue
rule.message: The expression 'a whole other' can seem colloquial. Consider replacing it with a more formal alternative. an entirely different \4

rule.name: This will allow (allows) them to bloom...
rule.message: Future tense may not be necessary in this context. \1 allows

rule.name: any more (anymore)
rule.message: Did you mean the adverb \1\2?

rule.name: AstraZeneca
rule.message: The pharma company AstraZeneca is spelled without a space in between.

rule.name: Christmas time (Christmastime)
rule.message: The noun Christmastime (= the Christmas season) is normally spelled as one word.

rule.name: decision making (decision-making)
rule.message: The noun \1-\2 (= the process of deciding something) is spelled with a hyphen.

rule.name: Do able (Doable)
rule.message: Did you mean the adjective doable?

rule.message: The adjective or adverb \1-\2 is normally spelled with a hyphen.

rule.name: every body (everybody)
rule.message: The pronoun every\2 is spelled as one word.

rule.name: a god send (godsend)
rule.message: This noun is normally spelled as one word. godsend

rule.name: above mentioned

rule.name: Comma after by default at the beginning of a sentence.
rule.message: Did you mean: By default,?

rule.name: Comma after a month

rule.name: redundant punctuation

rule.name: Enumerations with dashes: 1.2.-
rule.message: Dashes are unnecessary in enumerations. \2\3\4\5

rule.message: Wikipedia style. Replace "#" with No. or number when referring to numbers or rankings.

rule.name: Non-standard character in a word
